name: Hurakan
description: Hurakan, also known as Huracan, is a K'iche' Mayan god of wind, storm, and fire. He is one of the creator deities who participated in the several attempts to create humankind detailed in the Popol Vuh. Hurakan's name translates to 'one-legged', 'thunderbolt', or 'hurricane', reflecting his powerful and destructive nature. He played a crucial role in the creation of the world and the destruction of previous, unsuccessful iterations of humanity. He is often depicted as a powerful force of nature, capable of unleashing devastating storms and floods. He is associated with the heart of the sky and is considered a divine wind. He is one of the three creator gods, alongside Tepeu and Gucumatz, who planned and executed the creation of humans from maize. Hurakan's power is not solely destructive; he is also a force of renewal and purification, clearing away the old to make way for the new. He represents the raw, untamed power of nature and its capacity to both create and destroy. He is a vital figure in understanding the Mayan worldview and their relationship with the natural world.
subtitle: Heart of Sky, Wind, Storm, Fire

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humankind
keyMyths.description: Hurakan, along with Tepeu and Gucumatz, participated in creating humankind from maize after previous attempts failed. He played a key role in the destruction of the wooden people with a great flood.

keyMyths.title: Hurakan's Role in the Flood
keyMyths.description: As a god of storms, Hurakan unleashed a devastating flood that wiped out the wooden people because they did not honor the gods. This flood was a purification of the world.

keyMyths.title: One-Legged God
keyMyths.description: The name Hurakan translates to 'one-legged,' possibly symbolizing his swift and unpredictable movements, like a storm that appears suddenly. The exact interpretation remains a topic of scholarly debate.

sources.author: Dennis Tedlock
